coredata.dc:title: A general and simple method to disperse 2D nanomaterials for promoting cement hydration
coredata.dc:creator: He, Haijie
E, Shuang
Qiao, Hongxia
Yang, Jie
Lin, Caiyuan
He, Chuang
Xu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials can accelerate hydration of cementitious materials, but suffer from extremely poor dispersion. Up to now, it is still a considerable challenge to efficiently disperse 2D nanomaterials with a universal and facile approach. To overcome it, a general and simple ultrasonic treatment using graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted was created, which efficiently exfoliates and disperses 2D nanomaterials including graphene oxide (GO), calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH) and g-C3N4 (CN), and these as-formed well dispersed 2D nanomaterials served as additives to accelerate cement hydration for the first time. Crucially, the related dispersity mechanism and hydration acceleration mechanism were comprehensively revealed. In detail, GO, CLDH, and CN treated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication (namely, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN) with higher dispersity possessed larger specific surface areas, increased by 94.35%, 70.22% and 75.48% than GO, CLDH, and CN treated by pure ultrasonication (i.e., U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN). According to characteristics of these well dispersed 2D nanomaterials, the intercalation effect, exfoliation effect, and surfactant effect of GQDs were identified as the dispersity mechanism of GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment. More importantly, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN improved hydration, mechanical and microstructural properties of cement composites more significantly than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively. In detail, compared with the mortar without the addition of 2D nanomaterials, 0.02 wt% G-GO, 0.5 wt% G-CLDH, and 0.5 wt% G-CN improved the compressive/flexural strength of 7-d curing mortars by 33.9%/24.6%, 26.6%/14.8%, and 25%/13.1% respectively. The hydration acceleration mechanism was involved in the more nucleation sites being provided by highly dispersed 2D nanomaterials. This work would contribute to overcoming the obstacle of poor dispersity of 2D nanomaterials, thereby effectively employing 2D nanomaterials to enhance properties of cement composites.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials can accelerate hydration of cementitious materials, but suffer from extremely poor dispersion. Up to now, it is still a considerable challenge to efficiently disperse 2D nanomaterials with a universal and facile approach. To overcome it, a general and simple ultrasonic treatment using graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted was created, which efficiently exfoliates and disperses 2D nanomaterials including graphene oxide (GO), calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH) and g-C3N4 (CN), and these as-formed well dispersed 2D nanomaterials served as additives to accelerate cement hydration for the first time. Crucially, the related dispersity mechanism and hydration acceleration mechanism were comprehensively revealed. In detail, GO, CLDH, and CN treated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication (namely, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN) with higher dispersity possessed larger specific surface areas, increased by 94.35%, 70.22% and 75.48% than GO, CLDH, and CN treated by pure ultrasonication (i.e., U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN). According to characteristics of these well dispersed 2D nanomaterials, the intercalation effect, exfoliation effect, and surfactant effect of GQDs were identified as the dispersity mechanism of GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment. More importantly, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN improved hydration, mechanical and microstructural properties of cement composites more significantly than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively. In detail, compared with the mortar without the addition of 2D nanomaterials, 0.02 wt% G-GO, 0.5 wt% G-CLDH, and 0.5 wt% G-CN improved the compressive/flexural strength of 7-d curing mortars by 33.9%/24.6%, 26.6%/14.8%, and 25%/13.1% respectively. The hydration acceleration mechanism was involved in the more nucleation sites being provided by highly dispersed 2D nanomaterials. This work would contribute to overcoming the obstacle of poor dispersity of 2D nanomaterials, thereby effectively employing 2D nanomaterials to enhance properties of cement composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cement composites, as multiphase and multi-scale composite materials, are extensively employed in civil engineering [1–3], benefitting from their low cost, superior mechanical qualities and great fire resistance. However, they are still subject to internal multi-scale pores and interfacial microcracks, extremely detrimental to their mechanical strength and durability [4]. Nano-engineering is one of the most effective strategies to address the aforementioned problems [5], by incorporating nanomaterials into cementitious materials. Up to now, the microstructure of cement composites can be refined and hydration accelerated by numerous nanomaterials, including SiO2 [6–8], CaCO3 [7,9,10], TiO2 [9,11,12], Al2O3 [13–15], carbon nanotubes [16–18], graphene oxide (GO) [19–21], calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH) [22], and g-C3N4 (CN) [23]. For instance, Kong et al. investigated the effect of the oxygen-containing functional group level of reduced GO on the performance of cement composites, and revealed that reduced GO with mild oxygen group level was conducive to enhancing the durability of mortars [21]. While Yang et al. found that the micro-filler effect of polymeric CN contributed to reducing harmful pores of cementitious materials, thereby increasing their compressive strength [23]. Generally, these nanomaterials can be divided into zero-dimensional (0D) particles, one-dimensional (1D) wires/robs/tubes and two-dimensional (2D) sheets based on their morphologies. Among them, 2D nanomaterials with unique single-/multi-layer lamellar structures can promote hydration and enhance fracture bridging capacity, thereby substantially densifying microstructure and reducing microcrack extension of cementitious materials [24]. Thus, the related research has attracted intensive interest in recent decades.

Despite tremendous progress, the large-scale deployment of 2D nanomaterials in cement-based materials is confronted with an enormous challenge of poor dispersity, severely limiting their positive effects [25,26]. As is well known, the dispersion degree of 2D nanomaterials is directly related to the augmentation of cement matrix [26,27]. A great dispersity degree tends to tremendously enhance the mechanics and durability of cementitious materials. In other words, the agglomeration of 2D nanomaterials enable them to fail to fully exert benefits such as the nucleation and crack bridging effects, leading to an unfavorable impact on the performance of cementitious materials. Furthermore, to mitigate their agglomeration-induced negative influence, more 2D nanomaterials need to be incorporated compensatively, resulting in a sharp increase in cost. Therefore, multitudes of techniques have been developed to improve the dispersity of 2D nanomaterials. These techniques fall into physical and chemical modification categories. The former mainly includes ultrasonic treatment [28,29]. For example, Lv et al. employed the ultrasonic method to obtain the stable GO nanosheets dispersion aqueous solution for enhancing toughness of cement matrices [29]. While the latter involves surfactant addition [30,31], chemical grafting [32,33], and functional group modification [34,35]. For instance, Lv’s group used a copolymerization way to fabricate polycarboxylate/graphene oxide nanosheets (GONs) composites, endowing GONs with smaller size and more uniform dispersity [32]. Despite significant advancements in 2D nanomaterials dispersion technologies, several limitations are unavoidable: (1) Lack of generality. Existing dispersion methods above are only applied to disperse specific 2D nanomaterials. In detail, chemical grafting and functional group modification require the adjustment of reagents and dispersion processes according to the type of 2D nanomaterials. (2) Complicated operation. Chemical modification ways including chemical grafting and functional group modification frequently involve more complicated operating processes and more expensive materials, raising the cost of dispersing nanomaterials dramatically. (3) Poor dispersity efficiency. Physical dispersion approaches share the low dispersion efficiency, and it is difficult to complete the uniform dispersion of 2D nanomaterials. More importantly, these methods cannot prevent reaggregation, especially in alkaline and complicated ionic conditions of cement composites [25,36]. (4) Pollution and safety risks. Although chemical modification can considerably improve the dispersibility of 2D nanomaterials, auxiliary hazardous reagents such as NaHSO3 [32] and triethanolamine [33] are introduced, giving rise to environmental contamination and potential safety risks. Hence, it is highly desirable to design a universal, facile and environmentally friendly dispersion method to efficiently disperse 2D nanomaterials for the performance improvement of cement composites.

Recently, it has been demonstrated that amphiphilic dispersants such as lignin [37] and pyrene derivatives [38] have been employed to insert and interact with layered structures of 2D nanomaterials under ultrasound treatment, resulting in efficient dispersion of 2D nanomaterials [39]. However, the above-mentioned method still has the unsatisfied efficiencies and tends to cause environmental concern. Exhilaratingly, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), acting as a newly discovered carbon nanomaterial, can be considered as a giant amphiphilic polyaromatic molecule [40]. It must be pointed out that GQDs have been widely used in optoelectronic devices, biomedicine, sensing, catalysis, lubrication, and corrosion inhibition due to their facile and low-cost synthesis, superior water solubility and environmentally friendly [41–46]. If GQDs can function as a new amphiphilic dispersant to intercalate into and interact with 2D nanomaterials more effectively, 2D nanomaterials will be more efficaciously and environmentally exfoliated and dispersed from bulk materials. Indeed, the latest literature has proved that GQDs can effectively exfoliate MoS2, h-BN or WS2 in water/ethanol mixture under low-power bath sonication toward enhancing electrocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution reaction [47,48]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the applicability and universality of GQDs for the efficient dispersion of typical 2D nanomaterials (GO, CLDH, and CN) to accelerate cement hydration have not been studied.

In this work, a general and simple GQDs-aided ultrasonic method was developed to efficiently exfoliate and disperse typical 2D nanomaterials (GO, CLDH, and CN), and as-formed high-dispersion 2D nanomaterials (i.e., G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN) were investigated for the first time to accelerate cement hydration. More importantly, the related dispersity mechanism and hydration acceleration mechanism were rationally uncovered. Specifically, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were performed to compare the dispersed behaviors of GO, CLDH, and CN treated by ultrasonication with or without GQDs, confirming that the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment of on 2D nanomaterials had a favorable dispersity effect. Crucially, the influence of different 2D nanomaterials on hydration, mechanical and microstructural properties of cement composites was systematically investigated. And based on characterization of various 2D nanomaterials and cement-based materials, the dispersity mechanism of GQDs-assisted ultrasonication, and hydration promotion mechanism by G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN were revealed. This work would contribute to improving the performance of cement composites by efficiently applying 2D nanomaterials to solve the problem of poor dispersity of 2D nanomaterials.

Cement-based specimens were made using Portland cement (PC) with the type of PI 52.5 R grade aligning with Chinese standards of GB175 and GB176. The physical and chemical characteristics of PC were presented in Table 1 and Table 2. Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) supplied the urea, commercial graphite oxide powder, and ultrapure water. Carbonate type magnesium aluminium layered double hydroxides (Mg/Al-CO3 LDH) were obtained from Heowns Opde Technologies, Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Graphite rods were purchased from Jing Xi Tan Su Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). None of the materials required additional purification before being used. All the cement-related experiments were executed using deionized (DI) water.

GQDs were prepared by electrochemically etching graphite according to the reported literature [49] with a marginal variation. Specifically, two graphite rods (a purity of 99.99%, 15 cm in length and 0.6 cm in diameter) with a separation of 6 cm served as the anode and cathode, and then were parallelly immersed in a beaker filled with 800 mL ultrapure water. To promote the electrochemical reaction, some as-prepared GQDs were added into the beaker to enhance the conductivity of the ultrapure water. Under continuous magnetic stirring, 30 V voltage provided by a direct current power supply was applied to the two electrodes for 24 h. After that, the anode graphite rod was etched to produce a black solution. Subsequently, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 12 000 rpm for 10 min to remove precipitated graphite particles and graphite oxide, following by filtration through a 0.2 μm membrane, rotary evaporation and freeze-drying to obtain black powdered GQDs.

Regarding GO, commercial graphite oxide powder ( Fig. 1 a) was used as the precursor, and it was created by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. Additionally, consecutive tip-sonication with the parameters of 360 W and 20 kHz was used to provide the ultrasonication. In the TEM image (Fig. 1 d), the graphite oxide showed multiple layers. With respect to powdered calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH; Mg6Al2O9) (Fig. 1 b), it was synthesized by calcining commercial Mg/Al-CO3 LDH (Mg6Al2(OH)16CO3⋅4 H2O) in a muffle furnace at 500 °C for 3 h, in light of the reported reference [22]. TEM image (Fig. 1 e) revealed severe agglomeration of the CLDH particles, heavily stacked and clustered together. Referring to g-C3N4 (CN), urea was calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 2 h at a rate of 10 °C/min to create CN according to the previous report [50]. To make it easier to use, the yellowish CN product was ground into powder (Fig. 1 c). The TEM image (Fig. 1 f) illustrated that CN particles were agglomerated, leading to an increase in their size to several micrometers.

The simple GQDs-aided ultrasonic method was employed to exfoliate and disperse three typical 2D nanomaterials including GO, CLDH, and CN. The obtained dispersed 2D nanomaterials were named as G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN. Specifically, 500 mL of DI water was mixed with graphite oxide/CLDH/CN and GQDs at a mass ratio of 50:1. Subsequent tip-sonication (360 W, 20 kHz) was then performed in a water bath at 10°C for 1 h. Following that, cement-based specimens were prepared directly using the mixed solution; The other went through vacuum drying to obtain powdered GQDs-assisted ultrasonication-exfoliation 2D nanomaterials for characterization. U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN shared same preparation processes as G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN separately, only without the incorporation of GQDs ( Fig. 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and structures of GQDs and various 2D nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of GQDs and different 2D nanomaterials were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images taken on FEI Talos F200S (USA) and Brucker MFP-3D infinity (Germany), respectively. Fourier infrared (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA), Raman (Thermo Scientific DXR, USA), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Brucker D8 Advance, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250, USA) spectra were used to characterize the structure information. A Micromertics Quantachrome instrument with the type of NOVA4200E (USA) was employed to conduct N2 adsorption-desorption testing, and then assess the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of different 2D nanomaterials. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra obtained by a PerkinElmer Lambda 750 instrument (USA) were used to investigate the dispersion characteristics of diverse 2D nanomaterials in DI water and saturated calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2; CH) solution.

To investigate their effect on cement matrix, six kinds of 2D nanomaterials (i.e., U-GO, U-CLDH, U-CN, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN) were added to specimens respectively under the same conditions. The specimens were prepared in the manner described below ( Fig. 3): Firstly, the obtained mixed solution containing U-GO, U-CLDH, U-CN, G-GO, G-CLDH, or G-CN was incorporated into mixture of cement and sand. Among them, the addition of U-GO or G-GO was 0.02 wt% (by weight of cement), while with reference of U-CLDH, U-CN, G-CLDH, or G-CN, the incorporation was 0.5 wt%. The water-cement ratio (w/c) was 0.5, and the sand-cement ratio was 3.0. It should be mentioned that the dosage of 2D nanomaterials was selected based on the best modification effect of previous reports [19,22,23]. Following by mechanical stirring, the mortar was poured into molds of 160 mm × 40 mm × 40 mm. Subsequently, these specimens were placed in a curing chamber with a relative humidity of more than 95% and a temperature at 20 ± 2 °C, and then demoulded after 24 h, finally cured until the beginning of corresponding tests. The mix proportions were displayed in Table 3. The control mortar sample was denoted by “C” without the addition of 2D nanomaterials. And other mortars containing different 2D nanomaterials were labeled as X-M (X corresponded to the name of the 2D nanomaterial). Besides, to analyze the influence of various 2D nanomaterials on hydration processes and microstructures, paste samples were fabricated using the same preparation processes as mortar specimens only without the incorporation of sand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties, phase compositions and microstructures of different samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanical analyses of different mortars were conformed with Chinese standard GB/T 17671–2021. After 7-d curing period, tests were conducted on mortar specimens to determine their flexural and compressive strength. To better understand the underlying influence of various 2D nanomaterials on cement hydration of cement, XRD, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), scanning electron microscope (SEM) and N2 adsorption-desorption testing were conducted to analyze phase compositions and microstructures of different specimens. As for TGA, the testing sample was prepared as follows: After being exposed to anhydrous ethanol for 7 d to terminate hydration process, the specimens of 7-d curing cement paste were vacuum dried at 50 ℃ until their mass remained unchanged, and they were then ground into a powder with a diameter of less than 75 μm. Using a STA-409PC thermogravimetric analyzer (Netzsch, Germany), TGA curves with a temperature range of 30–1000 ℃ were measured in a nitrogen environment at a heating rate of 10 ℃/min. Eqs. (1) and (2) were used to determine the hydration degree (α) of various cement pastes based on TGA results [51,52],

The chemically bound water content was represented by W
B , and the mass losses of samples were measured in the following ranges: m 105 – 440°C, m 440 – 580°C, and m 580–1000°C, respectively. The conversion ratio of chemically bound water was 0.41 when CaCO3 was converted to CH, and the ratio of chemically bound water after cement was fully hydrated was 0.24. Regarding XRD, its testing specimens share the similar preparation process. XRD patterns were recorded by a D8 Advance high-resolution X-ray diffractometer (Brucker, Germany) adopting a Cu Kα radiation source at 40 kV voltage and 40 mA current under a 6°/min scanning speed. With respect to SEM, prior to SEM testing, all the testing specimens were sprayed with gold to improve the quality of SEM images. SEM images (FEI Scios, USA) were used to analyze the microstructures of 7-d curing cement pastes. The pore size distribution of hardened cement pastes was revealed through the application of Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) theory under N2 adsorption-desorption testing, which was performed using a NOVA4200E micropore instrument (Quantachrome, USA).

Fig. 4 a and b displayed typical TEM images of the as-synthesized GQDs. Evidently, GQDs had a uniform distribution, and their average diameter was 14.4 nm determined by randomly measuring more than 100 particles. The GQDs had a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the graphene (100) plane. This inferred that GQDs possessed the high-crystallinity carbon core composed of sp2 carbon. The excellent monodispersity of GQDs was further confirmed by the AFM image (Fig. 4 c), and it was excellent agreement with TEM observation. Furthermore, the topographic height of GQDs ranged from 1.8 to 2.7 nm, suggesting that few-layer GQDs were obtained.

To acquire the structural insight into the as-synthesized GQDs, a series of characteristics were performed. The XRD patterns of GQDs ( Fig. 5 a) presented a distinct broad peak around 27.5°, which corresponded to the graphite (002) plane. This suggested that the GQDs had partially defective/disordered carbon and a graphitic crystalline structure [53,54]. The finding of XRD patterns was further supported by Raman and FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the Raman spectra of GQDs showed two characteristic peaks at 1338 and 1575 cm−1, ascribed to the D band (disordered sp3 defects) and G band (sp2 carbon) respectively [55]. The intensity ratio of two bands (I D/I G) of 0.87 indicates that the GQDs had a high degree of crystallinity [55]. The absorption peaks of 3425, 1717, 1350, and 1251 cm−1 were visible in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 5 c), indicating the presence of −OH [56], CO [57], and C−O [58]. The excellent water solubility of GQDs was made possible by these abundant oxygen-containing groups connecting on the carbon core. Additionally, the CC bond is responsible for the peak at 1592 cm−1 [59], suggesting the sp2 carbon structure of GQDs and correlating with TEM, XRD patterns and Raman spectra analyses. The result of FTIR spectra was further testified by XPS measurement. The full range XPS showed two strong peaks at 284 and 532 eV, assigned to C1s and O1s respectively (Fig. 5 d). In the high-resolution C1s spectrum, three peaks were assigned to C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C–O (287.2 eV), and CO (288.8 eV) (Fig. 5 e). The C–C/CC bonds accounted for a dominant content of 67.8%, further manifesting that the GQDs contained abundant sp2 carbon. Besides, the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 5 f) could be deconvolved into two peaks: CO (530.5 eV) and C–O (532.1 eV).

In brief, the analyses from a series of morphological and structural characterization manifested that the as-prepared GQDs are comprised of the hydrophobic carbon core with high-crystallinity few-layer sp2 carbon structure and hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups connecting on the carbon core. This typical amphiphilic structure endowed GQDs with great potential for exfoliation of 2D nanomaterials [40,47]. In addition, GQDs with an average diameter of 14.4 nm and a thickness of 1.8–2.7 nm had a remarkable monodispersity.

To determine the dispersion effect of GQDs-assisted exfoliation on 2D nanomaterials, the morphologies of different 2D nanomaterials were observed using TEM. Regarding U-GO and G-GO, they displayed a characteristic 2D shape with wrinkled features ( Fig. 6 a and d). As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, U-GO prepared by pure ultrasonic processing from graphite oxide contained multi-layer non-exfoliated graphene sheets, indicating the low dispersion efficiency of pure ultrasonic treatment. Notably, the thickness of G-GO was greatly decreased (Fig. 6 d), leading to the fact that G-GO owned a remarkably increased specific surface area. Thus, GQDs-assisted ultrasonication presented a more efficient dispersion efficiency for graphite oxide-derived GO. In terms of U-CLDH and G-CLDH, they shared the hexagonal structure (Fig. 6 b and e). Obviously, U-CLDH tended to assemble, stack and densely aggregate (Fig. 6 b), while G-CLDH had a better dispersity with a less stack. Consequently, in the presence of GQDs, ultrasonic treatment could display a stronger dispersion effect on CLDH. With respect to U-CN and G-CN (Fig. 6 c and f), they possessed lamellar structures. It was evident that the edge of U-CN that was shorter than 1 mm was curled (Fig. 6 c). In sharp contrast, G-CN illustrated fewer layers and a shorter length below 200 nm (Fig. 6 f), implying that ultrasonic exfoliation could more efficiently delaminate CN with the help of GQDs. Besides, compared with U-CN (Fig. 6 c), the specific surface area and dispersion of G-CN were further improved by the addition of more pores and less winkled edges through the use of GQDs during ultrasound process (Fig. 6 f). It must be pointed out that possibly owing to the low electron contrast between the GQDs and G-GO/G-CLDH/G-CN [60] as well as low content and small size of GQDs, it was difficult to find GQDs coupling on G-GO/G-CLDH/G-CN. In a sum, GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment revealed a more propitious dispersion effect on 2D nanomaterials (GO, CLDH, and CN) compared with the pure ultrasonication, occasioning that G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN shared larger specific surface areas and higher dispersions than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively. Therefore, there are strong reasons to believe that high-dispersity G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN could provide more exposed active sites to accelerate cement hydration.

XRD patterns of 2D nanomaterials were displayed in Fig. 7. Regardless of dispersion method, the same type of 2D nanomaterials unveiled similar characteristic peaks, demonstrating that the same type of 2D nanomaterials exfoliated by ultrasonication without and with the addition of GQDs shared the identical structural characteristics. As for U-GO and G-GO (Fig. 7 a), there was one distinct peak at 10°–11°, ascribed to the (001) plane of graphite oxide. In comparison with U-GO, G-GO exhibited a broader peak, demonstrating that G-GO held a lower crystallinity. Regarding U-CLDH and G-CLDH (Fig. 5 b), they shared the same peaks at approximately 11°, 23°, 34° and 62°, consistent with the planes of (003), (006), (012) and (113) respectively. Differently, intensities of these peaks from G-CLDH were much smaller than those from U-CLDH, indicating that the crystallinity of G-CLDH was greatly reduced. Referring to U-CN and G-CN, they had two distinct peaks at 13° and 27°, associated to in-plane stacking of (100) plane and interlayer stacking of (002) plane respectively [61]. And the peak of (002) plane in G-CN possessed a weaker intensity compared to that of CN, showing that the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation endowed G-CN with a lower crystallinity. More importantly, compared with U-GO/U-CLDH/G-CN, G-GO/G-CLDH/G-CN had blue-shifted peaks, suggesting that G-GO/G-CLDH/G-CN owned a bigger interlayer spacing possibly due to the intercalation of GQDs. It should be mentioned that after exfoliation with GQDs, no diffraction peaks were observed, probably owing to the comparatively low content of GQDs.

FTIR were performed to characterize surface functional groups of diverse 2D nanomaterials. As show in Fig. 8 a-c, the identical class of 2D nanomaterials treated by ultrasonication without and with the assistance of GQDs owned the same absorption peaks, suggesting that regardless of ultrasonic processing, surface functional groups of 2D nanomaterials failed to be altered. In detail, G-GO and U-GO possessed abundant O-containing groups including −OH, CO, O−CO, and C−O−C [36], beneficial to improving their water solubility. G-CLDH and U-CLDH shared the groups of −OH [56]. G-CN and U-CN had the same groups of −NH2/−OH [62]. It must be mentioned that the absorption peaks of GQDs were not found, potentially as a result of its low content. On the other hand, Raman spectra (Fig. 8 d-f) illustrated the structural information of these 2D nanomaterials. Regarding G-GO and U-GO, their I D/I G values were 1.00 and 1.12 respectively, implying that the introduction of GQDs reduced the crystalline of G-GO. For G-CLDH and G-CN, their peaks shared a noticeable blue shift than those of U-CLDH and U-CN respectively, demonstrating the layer reduction of G-CLDH and G-CN [47]. In detail, the peak at 2798 cm−1 in U-CLDH reduced to 2701 cm−1 in G-CLDH; Due to the phonon confinement effect brought on by the reduction of G-CN layers, the peaks at 1501 and 2317 cm−1 in U-CN decreased to 1479 and 2216 cm−1 in G-CN respectively [47].

The specific surface areas and pore structures of different 2D nanomaterials were examined by N2 adsorption-desorption testing. Table 4 provided the calculated specific surface area (S BET), total pore volume (V Total), and pore diameter (D V) based on BET. Apparently, in sharp contrast to those of U-GO (40.32 m2 g−1), U-CLDH (16.15 m2 g−1), and U-CN (18.39 m2 g−1), S BET values of G-GO (78.36 m2 g−1), G-CLDH (27.49 m2 g−1), and G-CN (30.12 m2 g−1) significantly increased by 94.35%, 70.22%, and 75.48%, respectively. Correspondingly, V Total values of 2D nanomaterials exfoliated with the addition of GQDs shared increasing tendencies. This strongly proved that ultrasonic processing with the assistance of GQDs not only demonstrated the universality, but also held more efficient dispersity efficiency than pure ultrasonication regardless of the types of 2D nanomaterials, thereby conferring higher S BET values on them. The enhanced S BET was expected to increase the nucleation sites of 2D nanomaterials, thus accelerating cement hydration [63]. Furthermore, D V values of G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN markedly decreased by 63.08%, 28.52% and 67.54% than those of U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively, further revealing that the incorporation of GQDs could effectively exfoliated typical 2D nanomaterials.

It is well acknowledged that UV–vis testing is commonly employed to explore the dispersion stability of nanomaterials in a specific solution. And according to the Beer-Lambert law, the larger the intensity of UV–vis absorption peak, the better the dispersity state of nanomaterials [64]. UV–vis spectra of various 2D nanomaterials dispersed in aqueous solution were shown in Fig. 9. The same kind of 2D nanomaterials held similar absorption peaks, demonstrating that the addition of GQDs had no effect on the absorption of UV–vis spectra for 2D nanomaterials. While the intensities of maximum absorption peaks for G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN at 230, 205 and 340 nm respectively became stronger than those of U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN, verifying that regardless of types, 2D nanomaterials exfoliated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment shared a better dispersity [64]. This result of the N2 adsorption-desorption test closely matched this discovery.

Research has demonstrated that a strong alkaline and high metal ion environment within the cement matrix can worsen the agglomeration of 2D nanomaterials [64,65]. As a result, the dispersity behaviors of diverse 2D nanomaterials in the simulated concrete pore solution (namely, saturated Ca(OH)2 (CH) solution) were evaluated by UV–vis spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 10, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN had greater intensities of absorption peaks in stark contrast to U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively, thus laying a solid foundation for their employment in cement-based materials. This firmly verified that 2D nanomaterials exfoliated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment possessed a better dispersity, and were more competent to resist re-agglomeration in the cement composites, possibly related to the attachment of GQDs onto the surface of 2D nanomaterials. It must be noted that absorption peaks of all the 2D nanomaterials in the saturated CH solution were different from their aqueous dispersions, potentially owing to deprotonation in alkaline atmosphere [62].

To sum up, the characteristics above substantiated that the developed GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment exhibited a superior dispersity effect on 2D nanomaterials than pure ultrasonication. More importantly, this novel method demonstrated its convenience (only adding a small quantity of GQDs) and universality (successfully dispersing diverse 2D nanomaterials including GO, CLDH, and CN). Specifically, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN shared larger S BET values of 78.36, 27.49, and 30.12 m2 g−1, remarkably increased by 94.35%, 70.22%, and 75.48% than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively. And they also showed better dispersity not only in an aqueous solution but also in a strong alkaline solution, providing a prerequisite for their application in cement matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of 2D nanomaterials on properties of cement-based materials

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate enhancing effects of different 2D nanomaterials on early mechanical properties of cement composites, the compressive and flexural strengths of mortars at 7-d curing were examined. As presented in Fig. 11 a, all the 2D nanomaterials dispersed by ultrasonic processing without or with GQDs could considerably increase the compressive strength of mortars. In detail, in sharp contrast to blank group, U-GO-M, U-CLDH-M, and U-CN-M endowed the compressive strength with the improvement of 24.5%, 18.0% and 13.2% respectively. Exhilaratingly, the compressive strength of mortars was further enhanced by G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN, notably increased by 33.9%, 26.6%, and 25% in comparison to the control group respectively. Correspondingly, the similar results could be observed from the flexural strength test (Fig. 11 b). The addition of 2D nanomaterials increased the flexural strength of specimens. Compared with the control group, U-GO-M, U-LDH-M, and U-CN-M made the flexural strength boost by 18.0%, 9.8%, and 6.6%, respectively; While G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M resulted in increase rates of 24.6%, 14.8%, and 13.1%, respectively. These findings above suggested that due to their nucleation and bridge effects [19,22,24,63], all the 2D nanomaterials could markedly increase the early mechanical properties of cement-based materials. And benefiting from better dispersity of G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN obtained by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication, they more effectively exerted nucleation, filler and bridge effects, thereby leading to more improvement of compressive and flexural strength for mortars.

TGA was performed to explore the influence of various 2D nanomaterials on the cement hydration samples after 7-d hydration. In Fig. 12 (a-c), all the mass losses of different samples were divided into three main stages: 1) loss of bound water from C-S-H and AFt occurred ranging from 104 °C to 440 °C; 2) CH decomposed between 440 −580 °C; 3) CaCO3 generated by carbonation reaction underwent decomposition at 580−1000 °C [22]. Compared to the control group, 2D nanomaterials-incorporating groups possessed stronger mass loss peaks. Thus, 2D nanomaterials increased the quantity of cement hydration products. And the incorporation of G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN with higher dispersity promoted more formation of cement hydration products than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN. Correspondingly, regardless of types of 2D nanoparticles, their addition could improve the hydration degree of cement compared to that of the blank group (Fig. 12 d). This was further demonstrated that the nucleation effect of 2D nanomaterials promoted the hydration process of cement. On the other hand, G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M achieved more improvement of cement hydration degree, rising by 0.23%, 0.67%, and 1.08% compared with U-GO-M, U-CLDH-M, and U-CN-M separately. This verified that there was a positive relationship between the dispersive degree of 2D nanomaterials and hydration degree of cement. It should be emphasized that since carbonization inevitably occurred in processes of specimen curing and test sample preparation, CaCO3 was converted to CH to determine the hydration degree of cement. The observations above showed that G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN with greater dispersity could promoted cement hydration process more effectively.

As is well known, the cement strength is significantly influenced by the degree of cement hydration. With R2 values of 0.92/0.90, Fig. 13 illustrates the positive correlation between the degree of cement hydration and compressive/flexural strength. Additionally, following earlier research, the compressive and flexural strengths rose as cement hydration rose [66]. This finding confirmed that the introduction of 2D nanomaterials contributed to enhancing the hydration degree of cement, thereby improving the compressive and flexural strength of mortars. At the same time, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN prepared by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment presented larger improvement of mortar strength, as their higher dispersity further promoted hydration degree of cement.

The XRD patterns supported the above conclusions from TGA. As illustrated in Fig. 14, regardless of whether 2D nanomaterials were incorporated or not, all the samples contained hydration product CH (2θ = 18.1°, 28.7°, 34°, 47.1°, 50.8°, and 54.3°), carbonated product CaCO3 (2θ = 29.4°), unhydrated C3S (2θ = 32.6°) and unhydrated C2S (2θ = 32° and 41.2°), demonstrating that whatever the classes of 2D nanomaterials, their addition failed to change the types of hydration products. In general, the intensity of major diffraction peak from CH at 18.1° is in proportion to its number, thereby used to evaluate the cement hydration degree. And a stronger intensity of the peak at 18.1° signifies a higher hydration degree [33]. Apparently, compared with the blank group, 2D nanomaterials-containing groups shared higher peaks at 18.1°, implying their greater cement hydration degree. And G-GO and G-CN conferred more conspicuous peaks at 18.1° on cement matrix than U-GO and U-CN, further reflecting higher hydration degree in G-GO-M and G-CN-M groups. Besides, G-GO-M group possessed the biggest CH peak among them, thus holding the highest content of hydration products, occasioning the largest mechanical strength. It must be emphasized that the CH peaks at 18.1° from G-CLDH-M and U-CLDH-M marginally increased, attributed to the G-CLDH/U-CLDH-caused pozzolanic reaction to consume CH [67]. And owing to the higher dispersity of G-CLDH, more CH was consumed, leading to relatively lower CH peak in G-CLDH-M group.

SEM images ( Fig. 15) were adopted to clarify the effect of 2D nanomaterials on microstructures of cement composites after 7-d curing. Regarding U-GO-M and U-CN-M groups (Fig. 15 a and c), the majority of CH showed the preferred orientation, detrimental to the mechanical strength of cement composites. This was possibly ascribed to the aggregation of U-GO and U-CN [32]. Besides, some inadequate and unevenly distributed C-S-H brought about the formation of some cracks, due to the undesirable dispersity of U-GO and U-CN. As for U-CLDH-M group (Fig. 15 b), owing to secondary hydration reaction induced by U-CLDH, no large amount of CH was found, resulting in the disappearance of preferred orientation. But multitudes of harmful pores failed to be filled. In sharp contrast to U-GO-M, U-CLDH-M, and U-CN-M groups, G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M groups (Fig. 15 d-f) markedly held more C-S-H, conducing to reducing the generation of cracks and macropores. At the same time, massive hydration products interlocked and interweaved, thereby leading to higher mechanical properties and more dense microstructures. All the observations above indicated that U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN with unfavorable dispersity could not effectively generate disordered CH and more C-S-H to fill harmful pores and cracks, giving rise to fail to refine the microstructure of cement matrix; While G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN with desirable dispersity could commendably refine the microstructure of cement composites through promoting the formation of more C-S-H and CH with random orientation.

BJH method was used to elucidate pore structures of different samples at 7-d curing ( Fig. 16). Generally speaking, the central peak of the pore size distribution reflects the density of cement matrix, and the smaller the central peak value, the denser the microstructure [22,68]. In comparison with that of the blank group, the pore size distributions of 2D nanomaterials-containing groups were significantly changed, and the central peak values were lowered. In detail, compared with that of the blank group (34.6 nm), the central peak positions of U-GO-M, U-CLDH-M, and U-CN-M samples shifted left to 31.4, 32.9 and 33.2 nm respectively. This was because 2D nanomaterials could fill pores, and also optimize the pore size distribution by promoting cement hydration. In terms of G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M, their central peak positions at 30.2, 31.6 and 32.5 nm created left shifts compared with U-GO-M, U-CLDH-M, and U-CN-M respectively. This demonstrated that their higher dispersity endowed cement with stronger hydration promotion, further densifying pore structures. In addition, G-GO-M group revealed the smallest central peak position, and minimum number of large pores of more than 30 nm. Thus, G-GO-M had the optimum mechanical properties. All the results above suggested that 2D nanomaterials were able to densify the pore structure of cement matrix. And the higher the dispersity of 2D nanomaterial, the better the pore optimization. Hence, G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M groups tended to hold the better pore size distributions than U-GO-M, U-CLDH-M, and U-CN-M specimens, precisely agreed with the conclusions of TGA and SEM. Besides, the refined pore microstructure contributed to enhancing the mechanical strength of cement-based materials.

In summary, in stark contrast to the blank group, 2D nanomaterials groups significantly boosted the compressive and flexural strengths, obviously promoted cement hydration process, markedly stimulated the formation of C-S-H, and refined the microstructure of cement matrix. And benefitting from their higher dispersity, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN could exert more positive effects on properties of cement-based materials than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersity mechanism of GQDs on 2D nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: According to all the characteristics above, the relevant dispersity mechanism of GQDs on 2D nanomaterials was proposed. That was the intercalation, exfoliation, and surfactant effects of GQDs ( Fig. 17). Specifically, GQDs prepared by electrochemical process possessed hydrophobic few-layer sp2 carbon structure in core and hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups connecting on the carbon core (evidenced by TEM, XRD, Raman, FTIR, and XPS), thus endowing them with amphiphilic property. Besides, GQDs were well monodispersed with a thickness of 1.8–2.7 nm and an average diameter of 14.4 nm (determined by TEM and AFM). These properties endowed GQDs with great potential to exfoliate 2D nanomaterials. In terms of 2D nanomaterials, due to their high specific surface area and high surface energy, they tended to be in a state of agglomeration. The cavitation effect produced by ultrasonication offered a strong tensile stress to overcome the van der Waals force among 2D nanomaterials, leading to the expansion of their interlayer space [69]. Thus, the as-prepared U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN shared improved specific surface area and favorable dispersity. However, the cement matrix held the strong alkaline and high metal ion environment, aggravating the re-agglomeration of U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN, resulting in their limited dispersity (proved by UV–vis spectra).

In sharp contrast, GQDs were able to adsorb onto the surfaces of 2D nanomaterials (GO, CLDH, and CN) through interactions of π-π or van der Waals force after their hydrophobic graphite structure was added [69]. At the same time, some GQDs were able to intercalate into the layered structure of 2D nanomaterials and adsorb onto layers due to their small diameter [40]. Thus, GQDs improved the cavitation effect between layers of 2D nanomaterials produced by ultrasonication by acting as intercalation and exfoliation agents. Simultaneously, GQDs provided a novel powerful electrostatic attraction force that resulted from the formation of H-bonds between the oxygen-containing groups of GQDs and water. These occurrences significantly improved the efficiency of peeling off monolayers from 2D nanomaterials, testified by TEM, UV–vis spectra, and BET. Moreover, after delaminating 2D nanomaterials, GQDs tended to couple on the surface of 2D nanomaterials, serving as the surfactant agent in this way to successfully prevent GO/CLDH/CN from re-agglomerating (testified by TEM, UV–vis spectra, and BET). All the GQDs-induced modifications improved the specific surface area and dispersity of exfoliated 2D nanomaterials (GO, CLDH, and CN). The GQDs-assisted ultrasonic method for dispersing 2D nanomaterials showcases several advantages compared to other established techniques. For instance, referring to ultrasonication alone [36], a commonly employed method, it lacks the enhanced dispersion efficiency provided by the GQDs-assisted approach. While the addition of surfactants [31,34] and chemical grafting [32] are alternative strategies to improve the dispersion of nanomaterials. However, these methods frequently involve more complex procedures or the use of additional chemicals, introducing variability and potential complications in the dispersion process. In contrast, the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic method stands out for its universality, implying its broad applicability across different types of 2D nanomaterials. Moreover, it demonstrates high efficiency in achieving a uniform dispersion, crucial for the homogeneity and performance of the resulting materials. The simplicity of this method is another significant advantage, offering a straightforward and easily reproducible technique that does not require complex chemical reactions or specialized reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydration acceleration mechanism of high-dispersity 2D nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the analyses of mechanical properties, hydration processes and microstructures into account, hydration acceleration mechanism of high-dispersity 2D nanomaterials was rationally proposed. That was high-dispersity 2D nanomaterials obtained by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication provided more nucleation sites to facilitate cement hydration ( Fig. 18). In detail, compared with U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN possessed larger specific surface areas and higher dispersions respectively. Larger specific surface area and higher dispersion of nanomaterials are known to provide more nucleation sites for the nucleation, precipitation and growth of hydration products (as known as the nucleation effect), leading to significant promotion of cement hydration [70,71]. As a result, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN could better exert nucleation effect to accelerate cement hydration, thus generating more C-S-H. This was supported by TGA and XRD. The more C-S-H in G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M groups refined the microstructures of cement composites, evidenced by SEM and BJH. This conduced to improving the mechanical properties of cement matrix, leading to larger compressive and flexural strength from G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M groups than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN groups separately. In other words, G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M groups could cause higher cement hydration degrees, thereby playing more significant impacts on cement strength. It should be mentioned that apart from hydration acceleration of high-dispersity 2D nanomaterials, their higher dispersions endowed them with better filling and bridge effects, also beneficial to the promotion of mechanical performance. Regarding the mechanical performance of cement matrices, the inclusion of G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN nanomaterials has shown to impart superior strength characteristics compared to those reported in other studies [31,34]. This improvement is notable under similar dosages of the nanomaterials. This finding is particularly significant, as it not only demonstrates the potential of these nanomaterials in enhancing the mechanical properties of cement but also highlights the advantages of the GQDs-assisted dispersion method in facilitating these improvements. In conclusion, this work firstly provided a general and straightforward method for dispersing 2D nanomaterials, and the as-formed 2D nanomaterials (G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN) possessed larger specific surface areas and higher dispersions, thereby offering more nucleation sites to facilitate cement hydration.

In summary, this work firstly testified the feasibility and generalizability of GQDs for the efficient dispersion of typical 2D nanomaterials to enhance cement hydration. Crucially, the related dispersity mechanism and hydration acceleration mechanism were rationally proposed. The main conclusions were drawn as follows.

(1) The typical amphiphilic structure (the hydrophobic carbon core with high-crystallinity few-layer sp2 carbon structure and hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups connecting on the carbon core) endowed GQDs with a promising ability for exfoliation and dispersity of typical 2D nanomaterials (GO, CLDH, and CN).

(2) The developed GQDs-assisted ultrasonic approach not only revealed a superior dispersity effect on 2D nanomaterials than pure ultrasonication, but also illustrated the convenience (only adding a small quantity of GQDs) and universality (successfully dispersing diverse 2D nanomaterials including GO, CLDH, and CN). Specifically, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN shared larger S BET values of 78.36, 27.49, and 30.12 m2 g−1, remarkably increased by 94.35%, 70.22%, and 75.48% than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively. More importantly, they showed better dispersity in a strong alkaline solution.

(3) Beneficial from their higher dispersity, G-GO, G-CLDH, and G-CN could more significantly boost the compressive/flexural strengths, promote cement hydration process, stimulate the formation of C-S-H, and refine the microstructure of cement matrix of cement-based materials than U-GO, U-CLDH, and U-CN respectively. In detail, compared with blank group after 7-d curing, U-GO-M, U-CLDH-M, and U-CN-M made compressive/flexural strength increase by 24.5%/18.0%, 18.0%/9.8%, and 13.2%/6.6% respectively. While G-GO-M, G-CLDH-M, and G-CN-M led to increase rates of 33.9%/24.6%, 26.6%/14.8%, and 25%/13.1%, respectively.

(4) Based on characteristics of diverse 2D nanomaterials treated by ultrasonication with or without GQDs assistance, the relevant dispersity mechanism of GQDs on 2D nanomaterials was ascribed to the intercalation, exfoliation, and surfactant effects of GQDs. While according to the analyses of mechanical properties, hydration processes and microstructures of different 2D nanomaterials-modified cement composites, hydration acceleration mechanism of high-dispersity 2D nanomaterials was ascribed to the fact that 2D nanomaterials obtained by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication provided more nucleation sites.

This work firstly provided a general and facile approach to efficiently prepare high-dispersity 2D nanomaterials, contributing to solving the intractable problem of poor dispersity from 2D nanomaterials. At the same time, the well dispersed 2D nanomaterials exerted a better promotion effect on hydration and mechanical properties of cement matrix, which advanced the use of 2D nanomaterials in cement-related applications.
